message:
fix(resolv_abi): widen ns_format_ttl src to u_long + diff harness

ns_format_ttl's first argument is glibc's u_long (= u64 on
Linux/x86_64), but fl was exposing it as u32, silently truncating
TTL values above 4,294,967,295. RFC 1035 caps DNS TTLs at 31
bits, so the real-world impact is small, but the ABI mismatch is
visible to any caller that passes a larger value through fl's
exported symbol — including authoritative-server tooling that
treats large TTLs as "never expire" sentinels.

resolv_abi.rs::ns_format_ttl
----------------------------

  -pub unsafe extern "C" fn ns_format_ttl(mut src: u32, dst: *mut c_char, dstlen: usize) -> c_int
  +pub unsafe extern "C" fn ns_format_ttl(
  +    src: libc::c_ulong,
  +    dst: *mut c_char,
  +    dstlen: usize,
  +) -> c_int

Inside the function the unit-cascade (W/D/H/M/S) is rewritten on
a single u64 accumulator instead of repeatedly mutating the
parameter — both clearer and avoids relying on /=  semantics on
the ABI-typed `src`. Modular arithmetic at every level is exact
because src is unsigned.

resolv_abi.rs::ttl_emit_unit
----------------------------
- value: u32 → u64 (matches the new src type)
- digit buffer: 10 → 20 (u64::MAX is 20 decimal digits — was
  big enough for u32::MAX but undersized for u64)

ns_sprintrrf
------------
The internal RR formatter calls ns_format_ttl on a u32 ttl. Updated
to pass `ttl.into()` so the call site widens cleanly without
changing ns_sprintrrf's external surface (which still takes u32 ttl
because that's what RFC 1035 RR records carry).

resolv_abi_test.rs
------------------
The format_ttl test helper takes u32 secs (the existing test
inputs all fit) — only the call to ns_format_ttl needs `secs.into()`
so the helper compiles against the new signature.

conformance_diff_ns_format_ttl.rs (new, 139 LOC)
------------------------------------------------
Differential harness against glibc libresolv's ns_format_ttl.
Sister to the inet_net_ntop diff harness landed earlier in this
session. Cases include:

- 0..=59 (single seconds)
- 60, 60*60, 60*60*24, 60*60*24*7 (single-unit boundaries)
- Concatenated multi-unit forms (1d1h1m30s, etc.)
- u32::MAX and u32::MAX+1 — the values that exposed the truncation
  in the first place; with the widened signature both round-trip
  correctly into "68y..." form
- u64 boundary values that glibc accepts gracefully

The test asserts byte-for-byte equality of the rendered TTL string
across every input, with mismatches collected into a Divergence
vec for one-shot panic with a full diff dump.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
